title: Beer and cheese quick bread
subtitle: A no-knead, no-yeast loaf with pale ale and mature cheddar
excerpt: A savoury loaf leavened with baking powder and ale rather than yeast. Mature cheddar and mustard cut through the malt. Ready in under an hour from bowl to board.
sourceNotes: Beer bread is a quick bread tradition linked to American Depression-era baking, where baking powder replaced scarce yeast. The cheese and mustard version is common in British farmhouse baking. This method draws on quick bread technique documented in Nigel Slater's Appetite (2001) and the beer bread canon established by James Beard in Beard on Bread (1973).

recipe.freezeNotes: Cool completely. Slice and freeze for up to 2 months. Toast from frozen.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best eaten on the day. Leftover slices toast well the following morning.

glossaryTerms.definition: A bread leavened with baking powder or bicarbonate of soda rather than yeast. The batter goes straight from the bowl to the tin with no rising time required.

content.text: A quick bread does not ask for patience. Baking powder does the work of yeast the moment it meets liquid and heat, which means there is no proving, no shaping, no knead. Keep the mixing short so the crumb stays open rather than dense.

Use a beer you would drink. A pale ale gives gentle bitterness and a golden crumb. A dark stout gives a denser, more complex loaf. Avoid very fizzy lager as the carbonation dissipates too fast to help the rise.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Grease and line a 900 g loaf tin. Combine the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{fine-sea-salt}}, and {{mustard-powder}} in a large bowl. Stir in 80 g of the grated {{mature-cheddar}}. Pour in the {{pale-ale}} and melted {{unsalted-butter}} and fold together with a spatula until just combined — a few streaks of flour are fine. This is a
: overmixing develops gluten and makes the crumb tight.

Scrape the batter into the prepared tin and scatter the remaining {{mature-cheddar}} over the top. Bake for 45 to 50 minutes until a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean and the crust is deep golden. An internal probe should read 90°C or above. Cool in the tin for 10 minutes, then turn out onto a rack.
